ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Coconut Chutney. Nearest parent is a coconut-based condiment or relish. A new concept for fresh South Indian coconut chutney is warranted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Condiments and sauces — coconut-based fresh chutney (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A12AC used as closest parent for condiments and sauces; no specific code exists for fresh South Indian coconut chutney.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for coconut chutney as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of coconut chutney is approximately 40 g (2 tablespoons or one small bowl), as typically served alongside idli, dosa, or vada at a South Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Chutney — derived from fresh coconut flesh and roasted chana dal composition data, ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN composition data for fresh coconut flesh and roasted chana dal, weighted by typical recipe proportions for South Indian coconut chutney. Sodium reflects added salt in a standard preparation. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry for raw coconut meat (FDC ID 170169) for macro plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Coconut Flesh — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; fresh coconut flesh GI approximately 45 adjusted downward for high fat and fibre matrix of chutney preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coconut chutney as a composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 18 based on fresh coconut flesh GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. The high saturated fat content (15.5 g/100g, predominantly MCTs) and dietary fibre (5.2 g/100g) substantially slow gastric emptying and attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 40 g serving = (18 × 11.5 × 0.40) / 100 ≈ 0.8, classified as negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (280 mg/100g); standard 40 g serving delivers approximately 112 mg sodium, offset by 88 mg potassium per serving
hypertension.guidance: A standard 40 g serving contributes approximately 112 mg sodium — well within the per-meal sodium budget for hypertensive individuals — and the 88 mg potassium per serving supports a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; however, portion control is advised if multiple servings are consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~18) and negligible glycaemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.8); high fat and fibre content slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coconut chutney has a very low GI of approximately 18 and a glycaemic load of less than 1 per standard 40 g serving, making it a suitable condiment for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed in typical serving quantities alongside a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (15.5 g/100g), predominantly medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) from coconut; cholesterol is 0 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Although coconut chutney is cholesterol-free, its high saturated fat content (15.5 g/100g, primarily lauric acid MCTs) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; a single 40 g serving contributes approximately 6.2 g saturated fat, which represents a meaningful proportion of the recommended daily limit of 20 g for a 2000 kcal diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lauric acid MCTs in coconut have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties; green chilli and ginger are recognised anti-inflammatory ingredients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh coconut chutney contains lauric acid-rich MCTs and anti-inflammatory spices (green chilli, ginger, curry leaves), which may provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut Chutney (தேங்காய் சட்னி / Thengai Chutney / नारियल चटनी) is the indispensable fresh condiment of South Indian cuisine — a smooth, creamy white-beige paste made by grinding freshly grated coconut with roasted chana dal (pottu kadalai), green chilli, ginger, and salt, then finished with a fragrant tadka (tempering) of coconut oil, mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and curry leaves. It is served in a small bowl alongside idli, dosa, vada, and uttapam, acting as the cooling, rich, slightly nutty counterpart to the tangy-spicy sambar. The chutney is very fresh and perishable, typically consumed on the day of preparation. Nutritionally, coconut chutney is energy-dense at approximately 212 kcal per 100 g, driven by its high fat content (18.5 g/100g), of which 15.5 g is saturated fat predominantly in the form of medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) including lauric acid. It provides 3.2 g protein, 11.5 g carbohydrate, and 5.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g. Its glycaemic index is very low (approximately 18), making it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. Sodium is moderate at 280 mg/100g, but a standard 40 g serving delivers only approximately 112 mg sodium. In Singapore, coconut chutney is available at all South Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and Indian hawker centres. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN composition data for fresh coconut flesh and roasted chana dal, adapted by recipe proportion weighting rather than direct laboratory analysis of a composite coconut chutney sample. Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central raw coconut meat data for macro plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for fresh coconut flesh, adjusted for the high fat and fibre matrix. No direct GI measurement of coconut chutney as a composite dish was found in approved sources.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for coconut chutney as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026; the dominant fatty acid is lauric acid (C12:0, saturated MCT). 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite chutney preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — fresh coconut and plant-based ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed by ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central data for coconut flesh. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — this is a plant-based condiment containing no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — this is a fully plant-based preparation with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based food with no significant vitamin D source. 7. GI value of 18 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement of coconut chutney exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Sodium of 280 mg/100g reflects a standard salted preparation; unsalted versions would have substantially lower sodium. 9. This record is classified as South Indian cuisine; multilingual coverage includes Tamil (தேங்காய் சட்னி), Hindi (नारियल चटनी), Malay (Chutney Kelapa), Chinese Simplified (印度椰子酱), and Chinese Traditional (印度椰子醬) per Project Veritas multilingual rules for Singaporean-context South Indian dishes.

improvement_actions_taken: Added Hindi local name 'नारियल चटनी' to local_names.hindi, flat aliases array, and aliases_by_language.hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for South Indian cuisine (Hindi mandatory).
Restructured per_100g field names to match RESULT template conventions (e.g., energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, etc.).
Added health_context.cardiovascular_disease as required by Q7 (not present in input).
Renamed health_context condition key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Added gi_source as structured object with title, organization, url, accessed_date, source_match_type, and source_match_notes.
Added nutrient_source as structured object per RESULT template.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) in notes with reason and source consulted.
Documented all 0.0 values for trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg in notes with justification phrases.
Added aliases_by_language structured object consistent with flat aliases array.
Expanded llm_training.natural_language_description to exceed 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Added foodex2_gap_note, snomed_ct_match_type, snomed_ct_gap_note to ontology section per template.
Set serving.is_default implicitly via serving object; set default portion to 40g per input.
